description: France recognized British rights to Rupert's Land, with some dispute as to the exact extents of the area. Great Britain had given sole trading rights in the Hudson Bay drainage basin the Hudson's Bay Company by Royal Charter in 1670
France ceded Acadia, excluding Île Royale (Cape Breton Island) to Great Britain. Due to difficulties in interpreting the actual size of Acadia, possession of present day New Brunswick was disputed.
France relinquished interest in Newfoundland, except fishing rights, to Great Britain.
St John's Island (PEI) and Cape Breton Island were annexed to Nova Scotia.
France ceeded New France, Ile Royale (Cape Breton) and Saint John's Island (PEI) to Great Britain.
France ceeded all of her territories in North America to Great Britain, except Saint-Pierre and Miquelon Islands.
France ceded Louisiana to Spain.

The Royal Proclamation described the boundaries of Quebec; assigned to Newfoundland the Labrador coast together with the Anticosti and Madeleine Islands.  Lands not contained within defined British colonies were reserved for the... Indians.
The Royal Proclamation described the boundaries of Quebec.  Lands not contained within defined British colonies were reserved for the... Indians.
Enlarged Quebec to include Labrador, Anticosti and Madeleine Islands, the Indian country both north and west to Rupert's Land and to the southwest to the junction of the Ohio and Mississippi rivers.
Independence of the United States recognized and boundaries between it and British North America described (imperfectly) from the Atlantic Ocean to Lake of the Woods
Colonial government established in Cape Breton

event: Russia lays claim to Alaskan coast
description: Grigori Shelikhov established a village on Kodiak Island and claimed adjoining coast for Russia
Colonial government established in New Brunswick
Opened northwest coast to occupation by any country
A Constitutional Act, followed by Order in Council, divided Quebec into Lower Canada and Upper Canada
USA and Great Britain both laid claim to land that will eventually become the Oregon Territory
In 1802 Spain ceeded Louisiana to France, and in the following year France sold Louisiana to the USA.
Re-annexed the Labrador Coast and Anticosti Island to Newfoundland
Convention: International boundary extended along 49th parallel from Lake of the Woods to the Rocky Mountains.
Convention: Oregon Territory to west to be administered jointly by US and Britain for 10 years
Boundary between Oregon Territory and the Spanish Territory established at the 42nd parallel
Cape Breton Island re-annexed to Nova Scotia
Anglo-Russian Treaty defined lines of demarcation between Alaska and British North America
Labrador Coast between Saint-Jean River and Anse Sablon and Anticosti Island re-annexed to Lower Canada.
United Upper and Lower Canada into Province of Canada. Provinces renamed Canada East and Canada West
Settled boundaries still undefined by Treaty of 1783: between Canada East and New Hampshire, New Brunswick and Maine
Settled boundaries still undefined by Treaty of 1783: between Canada West and Minnesota
Extended 49th Parallel to Pacific excluding Vancouver's Island. Northern area called New Caledonia.
A British-appointed commission failed to settle the dispute over the boundary between Canada and New Brunswick
Vancouver's Island established as a Crown Colony
Agreement between British-appointed arbitrators on adjustments to Canada-New Brunswick boundary promulgated by an Act of Parliament
A Bill to provide for the government of New Caledonia established new boundaries and the area was renamed British Columbia
Established British jurisdiction over land not in Rupert's Land or British Columbia which was called The North-Western Territory
British Order in Council created Stickeen Territory immediately north of British Columbia and south of the 62nd parallel

Western possessions amalgamated into British Columbia with present boundaries excluding Vancouver Island. Rest of Stickeen Territory above 60th parallel amalgamated back into The Northwestern Territory
British Columbia and Vancouver Island unit
The British North America Act united provinces of New Brunswick, Nova Scotia, and Canada into a federal state, the Dominion of Canada
British North America Act renamed Canada East and West to Quebec and Ontario

event: Saint-John`s Island separates from Nova Scotia
description: St John's Island established as a separate colony after absentee landlords petitioned for separation from mainland Nova Scotia.
Act of Dominion established Manitoba as the fifth province.

Following the Rupert's Land Act of 1868 - authorizing acquistion of Rupert's Land from the Hudsons Bay Company, the Britis Order in Council transferred Rupert's Land and the Northwestern Territory to Canada and renamed it the North-West Territories
Act of Dominion saw British Columbia enter the Dominion of Canada as the sixth province
Act of Dominion saw Prince Edward Island become the seventh province
New provisional northern and western boundaries of Ontario described
District of Keewatin created from part of North-West Territories

Imperial Order in Council: Transferred to Canada all British islands in North America not already in Canada, except for Newfoundland and its dependencies. The Arctic Islands were annexed to Canada as part of the North-West Territories
Manitoba enlarged by extending its boundaries westward, northward and eastward
Provisional Districts of Assiniboia, Saskatchewan, Alberta, and Athabaska established from parts of the North-West Territories and District of Keewatin
The south-western boundary of Keewatin is adjusted in 1886 to conform to the boundaries of the districts created in 1882
Ontario Boundary Act sees Ontario enlarged west to Lake of the Woods and north to Albany River
Ungava, Mackenzie, Yukon, and Franklin established as districts in the North-West Territories
Districts of Athabaska and Keewatin enlarged
Boundaries of Ungava, Keewatin, Mackenzie, Yukon, and Franklin slightly changed
Dispute began between the USA and Great Britain over the location of the boundaries between Maine and the bordering British colonies
St John's Island renamed Prince Edward Island
Dispute began between Lower Canada and New Brunswick over the location of their colonial boundary.
Yukon established as a separate territory
Quebec boundary extended north to Eastmain River
The boundaries of Yukon Territory in 1901 are changed to those of today
Alaska Panhandle boundary settled according to Award of Alaskan Boundary Tribunal, and Notes of 1905.
In 1905, Alberta and Saskatchewan are created as the eighth and ninth provinces with the boundaries they have today
Manitoba, Ontario and Quebec are extended to their current northern extents
Boundaries of the districts within the Northwest Territories are redescribed
Canada officially claimed a larger sector of the Arctic.
Boundary between Canada and Newfoundland is defined by the Imperial Privy Council. Canada accepts the definition, but Quebec does not accept the southern portion of the boundary.
Newfoundland narrowly votes to join confederation in a 1948 referendum, and officially becomes the tenth province the following year
Nunavut becomes Canada's third territory
Nunavut becomes Canada's third territory
